title: Proverbs 13
subtitle: Teachable Son; Speech that Preserves; Walk with the Wise

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — Teachable Son; Speech that Preserves; Hope Deferred

heb.paragraph: Hope deferred (tôḥelet mĕmuššākâ) makes the heart sick (v. 12) — one of Proverbs' most psychologically acute observations. Tôḥelet is not casual wishing but intense expectation; mĕmuššākâ means prolonged, drawn out. The proverb names what everyone has felt but few can articulate: delayed hope is a form of suffering.

Whoever guards (nōṣēr) his mouth preserves his life (v. 3). The participle form implies continuous vigilance — not a single act of self-control but a habitual posture. The mouth is portrayed as a city gate that must be watched, because what exits it determines whether life or ruin follows.

refs.note: The 'tree of life' motif appears in Prov 3:18, 11:30, 13:12, and 15:4, making 13:12 the third occurrence, not the second.
A wise son makes a glad father -- the father-son relationship anchoring v.1.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The first four proverbs of ch.13 create a character portrait by accumulation: the wise son accepts instruction; the good man eats well from his speech; the talkative person who guards his lips saves himself; the sluggard craves and gets nothing, the diligent is fully satisfied. Alter notes the accumulation method — multiple proverbs each adding a dimension — is the primary teaching strategy of the proverb collections.

'Walk with the wise and become wise, for a companion of fools suffers harm.' This is Proverbs' most direct social-formation proverb. Alter notes its mechanism: you become like the people you spend time with. Wisdom is transferable through proximity; folly is equally transferable. The choice of companions is therefore a wisdom decision of the first order.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The teachable-son opening connects to 12:1's teachability theme. Waltke notes ch.13 develops the social consequences of wisdom and folly: the wise person builds social capital (v.2: fruit of one's mouth), while the violent reap destruction. Wisdom is not merely an internal virtue but a social force — it shapes relationships, reputation, and community.

'Hope deferred makes the heart sick, but a longing fulfilled is a tree of life.' Waltke notes this is one of Proverbs' most psychologically precise observations — the physiology of hope and its frustration. The 'tree of life' image for fulfilled longing places genuine satisfaction in the category of cosmic blessing: to receive what you have hoped for is to taste Eden's gift.

Calvin: 'Some make themselves rich while having nothing; others make themselves poor while having great wealth. This is a paradox that ordinary accounting cannot explain — it is the spiritual arithmetic of covenant faithfulness. The man who gives generously appears to have little; he actually has much. The man who grasps for more appears rich; he is actually poor.'

Calvin on 'spare the rod, spoil the child': 'The father who refuses to discipline his son out of misplaced tenderness is not showing love but hatred — he is allowing his son to pursue the path that leads to destruction. True love for a child includes the willingness to cause pain for the sake of character formation.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'hope deferred makes the heart sick' discusses the word yachal (hope, wait expectantly). Deferred hope (mamshikhah, prolonged) produces sickness — a psychosomatic observation that is as accurate to modern experience as it was to ancient. The tree of life for fulfilled longing places emotional fulfilment within the cosmic framework of life and blessing.

The NET note on 'walk with the wise and become wise' (holekh et-chakhamim yechkam) discusses the sociological mechanism: wisdom is transferred through intentional association. The word 'walk with' (halakh et) means accompaniment — the regular, sustained companionship that shapes character over time. This is the positive version of the warning about companionship with sinners in 1:10–19.

hist.context: The first half addresses the teachable son versus the mocker (v.1), speech that preserves or destroys (vv.2-3), diligent versus sluggard (v.4), and the light/lamp contrast (v.9). V.12 is the section's emotional peak: wisdom acknowledges the real suffering of hope deferred before affirming that fulfilment is life-giving.

sections.header: Verses 13–25 — Fountain of Life; Walk with the Wise; Spare the Rod

heb.paragraph: The teaching of the wise is a mĕqôr ḥayyîm — a fountain of life that turns one aside from the snares of death (v. 14). The fountain image combines abundance with purity: living water is moving water, constantly fresh. Wisdom is not stagnant tradition but a life-giving current.

Whoever spares the šēbeṭ hates his son (v. 24). The rod is not a weapon of abuse but a shepherd's instrument of guidance and correction. The verse operates on the assumption that children left without boundaries are children who are unloved — discipline is an expression of investment, not anger.

refs.note: Fear of the LORD is a fountain of life -- the teaching-of-the-wise (13:14) and the fear-of-the-LORD (14:27) are aligned sources.
Bad company corrupts good character -- NT application of v.20's companion proverb.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The fountain-of-life and snare-of-death imagery for the wise person's teaching appears here in summary form. Alter notes this pair (fountain of life; snare of death) distils the entire Proverbs project: wisdom leads to life; folly leads to death. The extremes — life and death, fountain and snare — are not rhetorical exaggeration but the genuine theological stakes of ordinary choices.

'A good person leaves an inheritance for their children's children, but a sinner's wealth is stored up for the righteous.' The three-generation perspective is rare in Proverbs. Alter notes the observation functions both as a general principle about wealth accumulation (the prudent build across generations) and as a theological promise (ultimately, the wicked's wealth doesn't stay with the wicked).

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The second section develops the long-term perspectives that wisdom maintains. Waltke notes vv.13–25 are held together by the theme of future-orientation: the wise person weighs consequences (v.13–14), hopes for fulfilled longing (v.12), builds wealth gradually (v.11), and disciplines children for their future (v.24). Wisdom lives with one eye on tomorrow.

'Whoever spares the rod hates their children, but the one who loves them is careful to discipline them.' Waltke notes 'careful to discipline' (meaher musaro) literally means 'seeks their discipline' — the loving parent is proactively looking for opportunities for character-forming correction, not merely responding to crises.

Calvin: 'Those who despise the divine word are destroying themselves. The word of God is not merely advice to be accepted or rejected at pleasure — it is the expression of the divine will, and to treat it with contempt is to set oneself against the order of reality. The consequences are not arbitrary punishment but the natural outcome of choosing against the grain of how things actually are.'

Calvin: 'God takes care that the belly of the righteous is filled — not always with abundance, but with what is sufficient and appropriate. This is a covenant promise: the person who walks in covenant faithfulness will not be abandoned to starvation. The wicked may have abundance, but it is temporary and unblessed; the righteous may have little, but it is genuine and satisfying.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'wealth obtained by fraud dwindles, but whoever gathers money little by little makes it grow' discusses the Hebrew vathal (fraud, worthlessness, vanity) — the wealth gained by deceit or speculation. The note identifies this as a general economic observation: quick wealth gained dishonestly tends to disappear; patient accumulation tends to grow. The principle is both prudential and theological.

The NET note on 'a sinner's wealth is stored up for the righteous' (chote' tsafun letsadiq) discusses the eschatological resonance: the sinner accumulates wealth that ultimately transfers to the covenant community. The note cites Eccl 2:26 as a parallel (the sinner 'gathers and stores up wealth to hand it over to the one who pleases God') and Prov 28:8 as a third witness.

hist.context: The second half contains three of Proverbs' most quoted sayings: fountain of life (v.14), walk with the wise (v.20), and spare the rod (v.24). They share a common logic: formation requires active engagement with wisdom sources.

ppl.name: The wise son
ppl.text: Formation happens through relationship with the wise.

ppl.name: The mocker
ppl.text: Cannot be formed -- has closed the channel of correction.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Hope deferred makes the heart sick, but a longing fulfilled is a tree of life."NoteAmong Proverbs' most psychologically acute observations. The heart's sickness from prolonged disappointment is real and named.

src.title: Fountain of life
src.quote: Meqor chayyim appears four times in Proverbs (10:11; 13:14; 14:27; 16:22). Always a flowing source that enables life and turns away death.
src.note: The image emphasises wisdom as dynamic and life-giving.

src.title: Spare the rod
src.quote: The shevet (rod/staff) is the shepherd's instrument of guidance and protection.
src.note: The rod is guidance more than punishment in its primary register.

rec.text: Walk with the wise -- the basis for mentoring and spiritual direction.
Spare the rod -- among the most debated Proverbs texts in parenting literature.

rows.label: Walking with the Wise: Social Formation of Character
rows.text: Walk with the wise and become wise. The people you spend time with shape who you become. Wisdom is transmitted person-to-person in deliberate community -- and folly is equally contagious.

rows.label: Hope Deferred: Wisdom Names the Experience
rows.text: Hope deferred makes the heart sick, but a longing fulfilled is a tree of life. The wisdom tradition names the psychosomatic reality of frustrated expectation. The sick heart needs the living hope that wisdom promises.

tx.title: Opening line — MT vs LXX divergence
tx.content: MT reads 'A wise son heeds his father's instruction.' The LXX reads 'A wise son is obedient to his father, but a disobedient son will be destroyed' — substantially longer and more explicit. This pattern of LXX expansion is characteristic of the entire chapter.
tx.note: The LXX's expansion makes implicit warnings explicit. Whether the translator had a longer Hebrew text or composed freely is the central question for all of LXX Proverbs.

positions.name: Yes, within appropriate limits
positions.argument: The rod is a real rod, and the proverb is a real endorsement of corporal punishment as a legitimate tool of parental discipline. The multiple rod-passages in Proverbs (13:24; 22:15; 23:13–14; 29:15) form a consistent pattern that cannot be allegorised away. Applied wisely and in love, corporal discipline is the covenant parent's tool.

positions.name: The rod is wisdom's authority, not necessarily a physical instrument
positions.argument: The 'rod' in Proverbs represents the general authority and firmness of discipline, not necessarily a specific physical implement. Ancient shepherds used the rod to guide and correct sheep — the image is of authoritative, engaged, corrective guidance, which may or may not involve physical punishment depending on context.

positions.name: Culturally conditioned; the principle (discipline in love) is universal
positions.argument: The specific cultural practice (corporal punishment in a specific ancient Near Eastern context) is distinct from the universal principle (loving parental discipline is essential to character formation). The principle is non-negotiable; the specific application must be contextualised to contemporary understanding of child development and wellbeing.

prayer_prompt: Walk with the wise and become wise; a companion of fools suffers harm. Consider asking God about your companions — whose voice shapes your thinking most?

coaching.tip: 'A wise son heeds his father's instruction, but a mocker does not respond to rebukes' (v. 1). The contrast recurs: some learn from correction, others scorn it. The mocker has made a decision — not just to disagree but to dismiss. Wisdom requires humility; mockery presumes you have nothing left to learn.

'Walk with the wise and become wise, for a companion of fools suffers harm' (v. 20). Character formation happens through association. You become like those you spend time with. Proverbs isn't just giving advice about individual choices but about community selection. Choose your companions as carefully as you choose your path.
